sofienbergparken_subkultur.name: Sofienberg Park
sofienbergparken_subkultur.desc: A well-known gathering place for alternative communities, music, street culture and informal social life.
sofienbergparken_subkultur.popupDesc: For decades, Sofienberg Park has been a hub for subcultural communities in Grünerløkka. Musicians, artists, skateboarders, punks, rave communities and independent creatives have gathered here from the 1980s onward. The park has served as a space for concerts, small gatherings, political events and a lively street culture — a cultural free zone in the middle of the city.

middelalder_oslo.name: Medieval Park
middelalder_oslo.desc: A historic area in Gamlebyen with remains of medieval Oslo.
middelalder_oslo.popupDesc: Medieval Park is one of the clearest places to understand Oslo before the modern city center moved west. The area contains traces of churches, streets and ruins from the medieval town, and makes the older urban landscape visible inside the contemporary city.

oslo_domkirke.name: Oslo Cathedral
oslo_domkirke.desc: A church and historic center for city life and public gatherings through the centuries.
oslo_domkirke.popupDesc: Oslo Cathedral has stood as one of the city’s central religious and civic landmarks since the late seventeenth century. It has framed ceremonies, everyday worship, public mourning, celebrations and urban life in the heart of the city.

botanisk_hage.name: Botanical Garden, Tøyen
botanisk_hage.desc: Part of the Natural History Museum — a green breathing space in the city.
botanisk_hage.popupDesc: The Botanical Garden at Tøyen combines research, public learning and everyday recreation. It is both a scientific collection and an open green space where visitors encounter plants, ecology and natural history in the middle of Oslo.

torggata.name: Torggata
torggata.desc: A central street that has changed from a worn, mixed and partly neglected urban corridor into a more commercial axis with higher symbolic value, stronger branding and greater pressure from property, food service and nightlife.
torggata.popupDesc: Torggata illustrates how gentrification, regulation and property development reshape commercial streets over time. Physical upgrades, new restaurants, concept stores and stronger attractiveness have changed the street’s expression, use and reputation. At the same time, Torggata shows how market forces and planning power affect which businesses can stay, what rent levels become normal, and who gradually gets pushed out as a street gains status.

karl_johan.name: Karl Johans gate
karl_johan.desc: Oslo’s main street and national parade axis, where the city presents itself to itself.
karl_johan.popupDesc: Karl Johans gate is more than a shopping street. It links the railway station, the city center, the Parliament, the university, the National Theatre and the Royal Palace, and functions as a symbolic stage for ceremonies, demonstrations, tourism and everyday urban life. Here, power, public life and the crowd are organized in one of Norway’s most recognizable urban spaces.

radhusplassen.name: City Hall Square
radhusplassen.desc: A large open urban space in front of Oslo City Hall, where harbor, politics, festivals and everyday life meet.
radhusplassen.popupDesc: City Hall Square is one of Oslo’s most flexible public spaces. On one day it may be a windswept open square between the city and the fjord; on another it becomes a concert venue, festival ground, political meeting place or ceremonial urban room. Its strength lies in the way it can hold both official representation and popular city life.

bjorvika.name: Bjørvika
bjorvika.desc: A former harbor and traffic area transformed into a new urban landscape for housing, culture, offices and fjord life.
bjorvika.popupDesc: Bjørvika is where Oslo has most visibly rewritten its relationship with the fjord. Former harbor functions, road systems and logistics areas have been replaced by the Opera House, the library, museums, promenades, housing and office towers. The district is therefore both a showcase of urban transformation and a debate about property, public access, architecture and who the new waterfront city is really built for.

oslo_s.name: Oslo Central Station
oslo_s.desc: Norway’s main transport hub, where train, metro, tram, bus, taxi, retail and large pedestrian flows meet.
oslo_s.popupDesc: Oslo Central Station is not just a place of departure and arrival. It is a dense urban transition zone where mobility, waiting, shopping, meetings and social visibility merge. The station shows how transport infrastructure creates its own kind of city life, with both efficient movement and constant friction.

jernbanetorget.name: Jernbanetorget
jernbanetorget.desc: One of Oslo’s most important transport squares, where the city’s mobility becomes highly visible in practice.
jernbanetorget.popupDesc: Jernbanetorget lies in front of Oslo Central Station and is one of the busiest places in Norway. Train, metro, tram, bus, taxis, cyclists and pedestrians converge here in an urban space that almost never stands still. It is both an entrance to the city and a bottleneck where people constantly negotiate rhythm, direction and space.

bispelokket.name: Bispelokket / The Traffic Machine
bispelokket.desc: Former three-level interchange in Bjørvika connecting E18, Bispegata and Nylandsveien; a dominant road node and symbol of car-oriented planning later removed to enable Bjørvika’s transformation.
bispelokket.popupDesc: Former three-level interchange in Bjørvika connecting E18, Bispegata and Nylandsveien; a dominant road node and symbol of car-oriented planning later removed to enable Bjørvika’s transformation.

gronland_basarene.name: Grønland Bazaars
gronland_basarene.desc: Historic bazaar buildings in a diverse urban area where commerce, movement and everyday life meet, making visibility, belonging and boundaries especially clear.
gronland_basarene.popupDesc: Historic bazaar buildings in a diverse urban area where commerce, movement and everyday life meet, making visibility, belonging and boundaries especially clear.

ring_3.name: Ring 3
ring_3.desc: One of Oslo’s main traffic arteries, linking outer districts while also acting as a physical and mental barrier through noise, distance and neighborhood division.
ring_3.popupDesc: One of Oslo’s main traffic arteries, linking outer districts while also acting as a physical and mental barrier through noise, distance and neighborhood division.

trikk_17_18.name: Tram Line 17/18
trikk_17_18.desc: Cross-city tram lines through central Oslo and the inner east that connect homes, work, shopping streets and hubs, shaping daily urban rhythm.
trikk_17_18.popupDesc: Cross-city tram lines through central Oslo and the inner east that connect homes, work, shopping streets and hubs, shaping daily urban rhythm.

grunerlokka_helgesens_tm.name: Grünerløkka – Helgesens / Thorvald Meyers
grunerlokka_helgesens_tm.desc: Dense urban block structure with active street-level facades where sidewalks, storefronts and entrances create continuous social movement.
grunerlokka_helgesens_tm.popupDesc: Dense urban block structure with active street-level facades where sidewalks, storefronts and entrances create continuous social movement.

toyen_torg.name: Tøyen Square
toyen_torg.desc: Neighborhood square in the inner east where everyday life, waiting, chance encounters and low-threshold public presence take place.
toyen_torg.popupDesc: Neighborhood square in the inner east where everyday life, waiting, chance encounters and low-threshold public presence take place.

majorstuen_krysset.name: Majorstuen Intersection
majorstuen_krysset.desc: One of Oslo’s busiest intersections where trams, buses, cars and large pedestrian flows create constant movement, waiting and shortcut behavior.
majorstuen_krysset.popupDesc: One of Oslo’s busiest intersections where trams, buses, cars and large pedestrian flows create constant movement, waiting and shortcut behavior.

st_hanshaugen_park.name: St. Hanshaugen Park
st_hanshaugen_park.desc: Hilltop park and key green breathing space in the inner city, used both for recreation and as an everyday passage between neighborhoods.
st_hanshaugen_park.popupDesc: Hilltop park and key green breathing space in the inner city, used both for recreation and as an everyday passage between neighborhoods.

vulkan_energisentral.name: Vulkan Energy Center
vulkan_energisentral.desc: Early flagship project in green urban development: a hidden technical plant that supplies the transformed Vulkan area with local, flexible energy.
vulkan_energisentral.popupDesc: Early flagship project in green urban development: a hidden technical plant that supplies the transformed Vulkan area with local, flexible energy.

aker_brygge.name: Aker Brygge
aker_brygge.desc: Former shipyard and industrial site transformed into retail, dining, offices, housing and waterfront recreation; a key case in Oslo’s fjord-city redevelopment.
aker_brygge.popupDesc: Former shipyard and industrial site transformed into retail, dining, offices, housing and waterfront recreation; a key case in Oslo’s fjord-city redevelopment.

tigeren.name: The Tiger Statue
tigeren.desc: Statue outside Oslo Central Station symbolizing the “Tiger City,” and one of central Oslo’s best-known meeting points.
tigeren.popupDesc: Statue outside Oslo Central Station symbolizing the “Tiger City,” and one of central Oslo’s best-known meeting points.

gronland_kirke.name: Grønland Church
gronland_kirke.desc: Oslo’s first brick church on the east side, a landmark in a historic working-class district and a visible older institution in a changing area.
gronland_kirke.popupDesc: Oslo’s first brick church on the east side, a landmark in a historic working-class district and a visible older institution in a changing area.

kampen_kirke.name: Kampen Church
kampen_kirke.desc: Nineteenth-century church placed high above Kampen, serving as landmark, orientation point and identity-bearing structure in the neighborhood.
kampen_kirke.popupDesc: Nineteenth-century church placed high above Kampen, serving as landmark, orientation point and identity-bearing structure in the neighborhood.

oslo_bussterminal.name: Oslo Bus Terminal
oslo_bussterminal.desc: Main terminal for regional and long-distance buses in the capital, linking Oslo to the rest of Norway next to Oslo Central Station.
oslo_bussterminal.popupDesc: Main terminal for regional and long-distance buses in the capital, linking Oslo to the rest of Norway next to Oslo Central Station.

helsfyr.name: Helsfyr
helsfyr.desc: Major transport hub and office district in eastern Oslo where metro lines, bus routes and main roads meet in a commuter-oriented urban landscape.
helsfyr.popupDesc: Major transport hub and office district in eastern Oslo where metro lines, bus routes and main roads meet in a commuter-oriented urban landscape.

bogstadveien.name: Bogstadveien
bogstadveien.desc: One of Oslo’s main shopping streets, combining retail, dining, promenade and social urban life.
bogstadveien.popupDesc: One of Oslo’s main shopping streets, combining retail, dining, promenade and social urban life.

markveien.name: Markveien
markveien.desc: Lively Grünerløkka street with small shops, bars and cafés, often used to analyze commercialization, identity and gentrification.
markveien.popupDesc: Lively Grünerløkka street with small shops, bars and cafés, often used to analyze commercialization, identity and gentrification.

gronlandsleiret.name: Grønlandsleiret
gronlandsleiret.desc: Historic commercial street in Grønland where trade, migration, local networks and public life are tightly interwoven.
gronlandsleiret.popupDesc: Historic commercial street in Grønland where trade, migration, local networks and public life are tightly interwoven.

storgata.name: Storgata
storgata.desc: Historic central street combining commerce, tram traffic and intense everyday flow in one of Oslo’s major urban corridors.
storgata.popupDesc: Historic central street combining commerce, tram traffic and intense everyday flow in one of Oslo’s major urban corridors.

ullevål_hageby.name: Ullevål Garden City
ullevål_hageby.desc: Planned early twentieth-century residential area inspired by garden-city ideals, combining dense housing with greenery and human scale.
ullevål_hageby.popupDesc: Planned early twentieth-century residential area inspired by garden-city ideals, combining dense housing with greenery and human scale.
